Maternal health script

Biostatistician: Celine Hernandez, Mia Weller

Principal Investigator: Ali Jalali, PhD

2026-07-17

Study background information

The purpose of this study is to investigate the socioeconomic factors affecting severe maternal morbidity (severe) with an emphasis on economic costs of certain SMM. Using Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP) data from 3.9 million deliveries across 19 states (2016-2018), researchers examined individual and area-level predictors of SMM, maternal mortality, and delivery type. Researchers also evaluate healthcare utilization and costs associated with each subtype, assessing whether certain SMM phenotypes are associated with disproportionately high resource use. Our goal is for these findings to be utilized in healthcare policy reformation.

Aims

  1. Characterize demographic characteristics and clinical outcomes by SMM status
  2. Characterize demographic characteristics and clinical outcomes in women from High-High and Low-Low counties with respect to discrimination and socioeconomic status burden
  3. Using propensity score matching on patient characteristics and other relevant area-level factors, compare relative odds of SMM (overall and subtypes) and healthcare costs (overall and by SMM subtype).
  4. Compare distribution of SMM subtypes high-cost SMM patients (top 5th percentile) as compared to non-high cost patients.
  5. Map the distributions of High-High vs Low-Low exposure and prevalence of SMM.

Results

1. Characterize demographic characteristics and clinical outcomes by SMM status

Table 1a. Patient characteristics overall and by SMM status

The table represents the full cohort (N=3,923,323) of the participants. Among delivery hospitalizations, the median age was 29 years. Over half (53%) of patients were Non-Hispanic White, 17.4% Hispanic, and 15% Non-Hispanic Black. Most deliveries were covered by private insurance 51.6% and Medicaid 42.9%. Overall, 70.6% of patients had low comorbidity risk and 70,145 (1.8%) experienced a severe maternal morbidity condition.

Table 1b. Prevalence and cost of SMM subtypes amongst those with an SMM

The table includes the 70,145 deliveries resulting in severe maternal morbidity in the study. Of those deliveries, 62% experienced blood transfusion-related SMM. Other SMM conditions include disseminated intravascular coagulation (10.6%), acute kidney failure (6.4%), hysterectomy (5.7%), respiratory failure (4.6%), eclampsia (4.3%), and shock (4.1%). The remaining SMM indicators were observed in less than 4% of SMM cases. Respiratory complications are the most costly SMM subtype.

2. Characterize demographic characteristics in women from High-High and Low-Low counties with respect to discrimination and socioeconomic status burden

These tables display the crude (unadjusted) relationship between neighborhood discrimination and socioeconomic status burden, and severe maternal morbidity (SMM). Patients were categorized according to measures of racial discrimination and socioeconomic status burden in “High-High” or “Low-Low” groups. “High-High” represents patients from areas with high levels of racial discrimination and high socioeconomic burden (i.e., low SES). “Low-Low” represents patients from areas with low levels of racial discrimination and low socioeconomic burden (i.e., high SES). These groups were compared by patient demographics, clinical characteristics, and delivery outcomes.

High-High exposure groups included larger proportions of Non-Hispanic Black and Hispanic patients (21–31% vs. 3–9%), while Non-Hispanic White patients (68–74% vs. 33–38%) made up the majority of Low-Low groups. Patients living in High-High areas had higher proportions of Medicaid-served patients as compared to Low-Low areas which had higher proportions of private insurance.

Across discrimination measures, patients in High-High groups had approximately double the prevalence of SMM. For example, for the Black vs. White incarceration discrimination measure, the prevalence of SMMs amongst women from high-burden communities was 2.4%, over double that of women in low burden communities. Median healthcare costs were also higher in this group ($4,854 vs. $3,908). Similarly, within the Black versus white education measure the High-High group experienced higher likelihoods of SMM (2.3% vs. 1.4%). There were similar trends of higher prevalence in High-High groups for Black-White unemployment measures and Hispanic-White incarceration, education, and unemployment measures. Higher median costs were also experienced for High-High groups within these measures.

1 n (%)
2 Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Wilcoxon rank sum test

3. Propensity score methods: Compare relative odds of SMM (overall and by SMM subtype)

A propensity score analysis is used to estimate the effect of an intervention by accounting for pre-existing differences in the data set in order to mimic a randomized study. This will be used to determine how socioeconomic burden and racial discrimination will affect the odds of experiencing SMM. We propensity score matched on: (individual level:) maternal age, insurance, comorbidity index, year of delivery, (area level:) total population, median age, Medicaid expansion, RUCA code, quintile OBGYN density, quintile hospitals with birthing facilities, quintile hospital bed density, hospital number of births, state.

Results show that patients living in neighborhoods labeled “High-High” have higher odds of experiencing SMM compared to patients from “Low-Low” neighborhoods (p< 0.001). With respect to Black-White discrimination, the incarceration measure shows the strongest association, with “High-High” community individuals having a 64% higher odds of experiencing SMM (OR: 1.64) as compared to “Low-Low” community individuals. With respect to Hispanic-White discrimination, the strongest correlation was found in educational discrimination, with patients from “High-High” areas having a 72% increased odds of SMM as compared to patients from “Low-Low” areas (OR: 1.72).

BW Jail
BW Education
BW Unemployment
HW Jail
HW Education
HW Unemployment
OR (95% CI) P-value OR (95% CI) P-value OR (95% CI) P-value OR (95% CI) P-value OR (95% CI) P-value OR (95% CI) P-value
Overall SMM 1.64 (1.50, 1.79) <0.001 1.42 (1.29, 1.56) <0.001 1.57 (1.45, 1.71) <0.001 1.54 (1.41, 1.68) <0.001 1.72 (1.57, 1.88) <0.001 1.52 (1.41, 1.63) <0.001
Cardiovascular Complications 1.33 (0.81, 2.20) 0.258 0.87 (0.32, 2.41) 0.796 1.50 (0.61, 3.67) 0.374 1.03 (0.62, 1.72) 0.896 1.82 (1.08, 3.06) 0.024 1.13 (0.57, 2.21) 0.732
Respiratory Complications 2.04 (1.30, 3.20) 0.002 1.33 (0.56, 3.17) 0.514 3.13 (1.41, 6.94) 0.005 0.79 (0.47, 1.32) 0.363 1.87 (1.25, 2.78) 0.002 1.48 (0.85, 2.57) 0.168
Hemorrhage & Hematologic Issues 1.86 (1.65, 2.10) <0.001 1.41 (1.16, 1.72) <0.001 1.39 (1.15, 1.68) <0.001 1.01 (0.88, 1.16) 0.915 2.10 (1.85, 2.38) <0.001 1.30 (1.13, 1.50) <0.001
Neurologic / Obstetric Complications 2.20 (1.37, 3.53) 0.001 1.60 (0.84, 3.05) 0.153 2.31 (1.20, 4.43) 0.012 1.18 (0.71, 1.95) 0.522 1.54 (1.01, 2.36) 0.046 2.60 (1.43, 4.72) 0.002
Systemic Infections 0.83 (0.46, 1.51) 0.547 0.50 (0.09, 2.73) 0.423 1.17 (0.39, 3.47) 0.782 0.84 (0.43, 1.64) 0.612 1.17 (0.62, 2.19) 0.631 4.50 (1.52, 13.30) 0.006
Embolic Events 2.80 (1.36, 5.77) 0.005 0.60 (0.22, 1.65) 0.322 2.00 (0.60, 6.64) 0.258 1.46 (0.72, 2.96) 0.292 1.35 (0.76, 2.41) 0.309 0.56 (0.25, 1.27) 0.167
Renal Failure 1.93 (1.22, 3.05) 0.005 2.13 (0.92, 4.93) 0.079 4.34 (1.79, 10.54) 0.001 1.42 (0.84, 2.39) 0.191 1.81 (1.21, 2.71) 0.004 2.24 (1.26, 3.96) 0.006

3.5. Propensity score methods: Compare relative healthcare costs (overall and by SMM subtype)

For all deliveries High-High as measured by Black-White incarceration disparity (CR 1.10, 95% CI: 1.09–1.11; p<0.001) and education disparity (CR 1.05, 95% CI: 1.04–1.06; p<0.001) patients had higher total hospitalization costs compared to Low-Low groups. A similar trend followed for Black-White incarceration and education when limited to patients who experience an SMM. Hispanic-White education and unemployment linked to significantly higher total hospitalization costs in all deliveries while no difference as observed for patients with SMM. Amongst patients without SMM, High-High patients as measured by discrimination with respect to Black–White incarceration, Black–White education, Hispanic–White incarceration, Hispanic–White education, and Hispanic–White unemployment were observed to have higher total hospitalization costs.
